evidence: I started gravitating towards this theme of, like, a lot of people are talking about AI, but what does it mean for Human side... how do we ensure that as we have more automation in workplace, as we see more AI and adoption, how do we ensure that's actually good for us as humans
that's highly congruent [with what I'm working on]

We've built digital systems designed to filter *down* to the most relevant connections, but the moments that actually create trust are often the *unfiltered* overlaps... We optimize for efficiency, but trust is created by inefficiency. The 'small world' moments are valuable precisely because they weren't supposed to happen.

Desktop optimization is ultimate like you can rationalize it as being highly relevant because it's your day to day space but it is also such a like complete like luxury. Like a like it's an indulgence to like enjoy setting up your setup on some level than even productivity.
The monitor isn't about doing more—it's about being the *kind of person* who cares about the details. Maybe all productivity is just identity performance.

Vrijen described himself as becoming a '1% or 0.1% power user' of products... Yet he simultaneously identifies as 'non-technical but increasingly trying to push my boundaries'. Perhaps the most sophisticated users of any system are those who think in first principles about *how* the system should work, regardless of whether they can build it themselves.

Vrijen has moved 12 cities across 33 years... Perhaps frequent relocation doesn't just make you adaptable—it makes you *good at noticing* adaptation. The people who moved often may not be more flexible—they may be more practiced at recognizing when to stop trying to adapt and start trying to belong.

I have long been of the belief that, like, if you have substantive things to say, you don't need to rely on these like, cheap tactics... Authenticity isn't a competitive strategy—it's a *defensive* one.
One of my beliefs too is like, I mean for most part it's like collaboration over competition. I think there's just so much opportunity, especially for people who are looking at it from this angle.

We're not here to replace your product. We're the missing layer that makes your product defensible in the long run.

Handshake has 1,600 universities but no way to capture behavioral signal at scale. Careerspan has the coaching infrastructure to capture signal but lacks distribution. The combination creates a data moat neither could build alone. Real angle is data supremacy.

Access without signal is noise. Employers have 20 million profiles with no way to identify who will succeed. Career span has built the signal technology—the only platform that can tell employers who to hire, not just whom to reach.

Their core recruiting business essentially flat for [last year]. They're like moving away from that line of business. Positioning Careerspan as a strategic hedge for Handshake's declining core business... even if the partnership never becomes a major revenue line, it provides data supremacy and option value.
We're a three-person team. Like we're not even that expensive essentially to bring on. Being lean makes you an attractive acquisition target or partnership partner.
Dude, 20% increase, not over 90 days is material for the fact that I have not been posting. I'm not been doing shit... I've gotten 2000 followers in the last 36 hours.
